sections.content: Lesson 1: Hello & Introductions - The Basics of Meeting People

When you meet someone new, the first step is to greet them warmly and introduce yourself. In English, we use simple phrases like "Hello," "Hi," or "Good morning" depending on the time of day and the formality of the situation. Introductions typically follow a pattern: you say your name, and sometimes add a brief detail about yourself or where you're from. The most common formula is "My name is [your name]" or simply "I'm [your name]." These foundational phrases are essential in everyday communication, whether you're meeting a new classmate, a business colleague, or a stranger at a social event.

Understanding the context is important. A casual "Hey!" works with friends, while "Good afternoon, my name is..." is better in formal settings like job interviews or business meetings. Learning to read the situation and adjust your greeting accordingly shows respect and cultural awareness. Don't worry about perfection—native speakers appreciate effort and friendly intentions more than perfect grammar when meeting someone for the first time.

terms.definition: To say hello or welcome someone in a friendly way (あいさつをする)
To tell someone your name and basic information about yourself (自己紹介をする)
Following strict rules of politeness and ceremony; professional and respectful (形式的な)
Relaxed and informal; suitable for friends and familiar people (気軽な)
The circumstances or situation that influence how we communicate (文脈・状況)
The word or words by which a person or thing is known (名前)
A set pattern or standard way of doing or saying something (公式・型)
To recognize and value something or someone's efforts (感謝する・評価する)

sections.title: Key Takeaways for Greetings
items: Use "Hello" or "Hi" for casual settings; "Good morning/afternoon/evening" for formal situations.
Always say your name clearly and speak at a normal pace so people can understand you.
Smile and make eye contact—your body language is as important as your words when meeting someone new.

questions: What greeting would you use when meeting your teacher on a Monday morning, and why?
Practice introducing yourself: Can you say your name, where you're from, and one thing about yourself in 1-2 sentences?

sections.content: Lesson 2: Basic Questions - How to Ask for Information

Asking questions is one of the most important skills in any language. In English, we use question words like "What," "Where," "Who," "When," and "How" to gather information. The basic structure for asking yes/no questions is to put the verb before the subject: "Are you a student?" or "Do you like English?" For questions with question words, the pattern is: Question Word + Verb + Subject + Rest of the Sentence. For example, "What is your name?" or "Where do you live?" These patterns might feel awkward at first, but with practice, they become automatic.

When asking questions, remember to use a rising intonation (your voice goes up at the end) to signal that you're asking, not stating. This vocal cue helps listeners understand your intent immediately. It's also polite to add "please" or to start with "Can you..." or "Could you..." to make your questions sound more courteous. Native speakers appreciate when learners make the effort to ask politely, even if the grammar isn't perfect.

terms.definition: A sentence that asks for information and expects an answer (質問)
A question that can be answered with only 'yes' or 'no' (はい/いいえで答える質問)
Words like What, Where, Who, When, How that ask for specific information (疑問詞)
The rise and fall of your voice when speaking; the melody of speech (イントネーション)
Showing good manners and respect in how you speak and behave (丁寧な)
Respectful and considerate behavior toward others (礼儀正しい)
Your purpose or what you mean to communicate (意図)
Happening naturally without thinking or effort (自動的な)

sections.title: Question Structure Quick Rules
items: Yes/No questions: Verb comes first → "Do you like pizza?" ✓
Wh-questions: Question word first → "What time is it?" ✓
Always use a rising voice at the end to show you're asking a question.

questions: Turn these statements into yes/no questions: (a) You are from Japan. (b) She likes English.
Write three questions you would ask someone you just met using 'What,' 'Where,' and 'How.'

sections.content: Lesson 3: Personal Information - Talking About Yourself

One of the first things you learn in any language is how to share basic personal information about yourself. This includes your name, age, nationality, job or studies, hobbies, and family. In English, we use the verb "to be" (am, is, are) for many of these statements: "I am 20 years old," "My name is Sarah," "I am from Japan." We also use other verbs to describe what we do: "I study medicine," "I work as a teacher," "I like playing tennis." Having a prepared introduction helps you feel more confident when meeting new people or starting conversations in English.

When sharing personal information, it's important to know what to share and what not to share, depending on who you're talking to and how well you know them. With a new acquaintance, you might share your job and interests, but you probably wouldn't share your address or phone number. This is true in all languages, but especially important when communicating in a language you're still learning—you want to feel safe and comfortable. Practice introducing yourself in different ways so you can adapt to different situations.

terms.definition: Facts about yourself like name, age, job, interests (個人情報)
The country you are from or belong to (国籍)
Activities you do for pleasure in your free time (趣味)
A word that shows action or state (動詞)
Feeling sure and trusting in your abilities (自信がある)
A person you have met but are not close friends with (知人)
To change or adjust to fit a new situation (適応する・変える)
Free from danger or risk (安全な)

sections.title: Personal Introduction Formula
items: Name + Age/Job: "I'm [name], and I'm [age]. I'm a [job/student]."
Origins + Interests: "I'm from [place], and I enjoy [hobby]."
Only share information you're comfortable with—safety first!

questions: Write a 3-4 sentence introduction of yourself including: name, age, nationality, and one hobby.
If you meet someone, what personal information would you ask them about first, and why?

sections.content: Lesson 4: Numbers & Counting - Essential for Daily Life

Numbers are everywhere in daily life—they appear on prices, phone numbers, addresses, times, and ages. Learning to count confidently in English is fundamental. We have two number systems: cardinals (1, 2, 3, used for counting) and ordinals (1st, 2nd, 3rd, used for ordering or sequences). Cardinal numbers from 1 to 19 have unique names, while numbers from 20 onward follow a pattern: twenty, twenty-one, twenty-two, and so on. Understanding how to pronounce numbers clearly is crucial, especially when giving information like a phone number or price, since mishearing "fifteen" as "fifty" could cause real confusion.

Beyond just saying numbers, you need to know how to use them in context. When talking about money, we say "That costs ten dollars," using the cardinal number. When describing position, we use ordinals: "I live on the third floor." Practice saying numbers at different speeds and in different combinations—this builds both accuracy and fluency. Many language learners skip this "boring" part, but mastering numbers early pays dividends in every other area of English communication.

terms.definition: Numbers used for counting: 1, 2, 3, 4, etc. (基数)
Numbers showing position in order: 1st, 2nd, 3rd, etc. (序数)
To say numbers in order; to determine how many (数える)
The way a word or number is spoken (発音)
How much money something costs (価格)
When two things are mixed up or unclear (混乱)
The setting or circumstances that give meaning to words (文脈)
Ability to speak smoothly and naturally without long pauses (流暢さ)

sections.title: Numbers Matter
items: Learn 1-19 by heart—they're irregular and you'll use them constantly.
Ordinal numbers: Add '-th' to most numbers, but watch for irregulars like 'first,' 'second,' 'third.'
Say numbers SLOWLY and CLEARLY, especially when giving important information like phone numbers.

questions: Say these prices aloud in English: ¥100, ¥2,500, $19.99. Can someone else understand you?
What is the ordinal for these numbers? (a) 5 (b) 12 (c) 21 (d) 33

sections.content: Lesson 5: Days, Months & Time - Organizing Your Schedule

Understanding how to talk about days, months, and time is essential for planning, making appointments, and discussing schedules. In English, we have seven days in a week (Monday through Sunday), twelve months in a year (January through December), and we measure time in hours (0-23 in 24-hour format, or 1-12 in 12-hour format with AM/PM). When telling time, we say "It's three o'clock," "It's half past two," or "It's a quarter to five." Seasons (spring, summer, fall/autumn, winter) are also important for discussing weather, holidays, and activities.

One key difference from some other languages is that in English, we always capitalize the days of the week and months of the year. We say "I have a meeting on Monday" not "on monday." When discussing dates, Americans typically say the month first (March 15th), while British English and many other places put the day first (15th March). Learning these conventions helps you communicate clearly when scheduling meetings, making reservations, or simply chatting about upcoming plans. Practicing these regularly will help them become automatic.

terms.definition: A period of seven consecutive days (週)
A period of time with about 30 or 31 days; one of the 12 divisions of a year (月)
One of four periods of the year: spring, summer, fall, winter (季節)
Abbreviations for morning (AM) and afternoon/evening (PM) in 12-hour format (午前・午後)
30 minutes after the hour (e.g., half past three = 3:30) (半過ぎ)
15 minutes after or before the hour (e.g., quarter to five = 4:45) (15分)
A plan of activities or events with their times (スケジュール)
To write the first letter as a capital/uppercase letter (大文字にする)

sections.title: Time & Date Essentials
items: Always capitalize days and months: Monday, January—never 'monday' or 'january.'
12-hour format: "It's 3:15 PM" vs. 24-hour format: "It's 15:15" (military/European style).
Practice saying times: Use 'past' for minutes 1-29, 'to' for minutes 31-59 (e.g., quarter past three, twenty to five).

questions: Write out three sentences about your schedule using different days and times. Example: 'I have class on Monday at 9:00 AM.'
Say these times aloud: 3:45, 12:30, 6:15. Can you also say them in 24-hour format?

sections.content: Lesson 6: Family & Relationships - Describing People You Know

Family is often one of the first topics you discuss when learning a new language. English has specific words for family members: parents (mother and father), siblings (brothers and sisters), grandparents, aunts, uncles, and cousins. Extended family and in-laws (people who join the family through marriage) add more complexity, but the basics are what matter at this level. When describing family members, we use simple sentences: "My mother is a doctor," "My brother is older than me," or "I have two sisters." Understanding family relationships also helps you navigate conversations about home life, which is a natural topic between English learners.

Beyond just naming family members, you can describe them using adjectives. Is your sister funny? Is your grandfather tall? These descriptions make your English more interesting and personal. Remember that family structures vary greatly—some people have large extended families, some are only children, and some have blended families with step-siblings and step-parents. Use inclusive language when learning about others' families; don't assume everyone's family looks the same. This awareness of diversity will help you communicate respectfully in English-speaking environments.

terms.definition: A group of people related by blood or marriage (家族)
A brother or sister (兄弟姉妹)
A mother or father (両親)
The parent of your mother or father (祖父母)
A relative by marriage, like mother-in-law or brother-in-law (義理の関係)
A word that describes a noun (形容詞)
Family beyond parents and children: aunts, uncles, cousins (親戚)
A family formed when parents with children from previous relationships live together (再構成家族)

sections.title: Family Vocabulary & Relationships
items: Basic family: mother, father, sister, brother, grandparents, aunts, uncles, cousins.
Use 'my,' 'your,' 'his,' 'her' + family word: 'my sister,' 'your uncle,' 'his father.'
Add adjectives for description: 'My older brother is kind.' 'Her youngest sister is funny.'

questions: Draw a simple family tree and label each person in English with at least one descriptive adjective.
Write 3-4 sentences describing your family members. Include: who they are, what they do, and one personality trait for each.

sections.content: Lesson 7: Foods & Drinks - Talking About What You Eat

Food is a universal topic that makes for excellent conversation practice. English has a rich vocabulary for different foods and dishes. Basic categories include vegetables (carrots, broccoli, spinach), fruits (apples, bananas, oranges), proteins (chicken, fish, beef, tofu), grains (rice, bread, pasta), and dairy products (milk, cheese, yogurt). Beyond naming individual foods, you can talk about your preferences: "I like pizza," "I don't like spicy food," "My favorite food is sushi." You can also describe how food tastes (delicious, sweet, salty, bitter, sour) and how it's prepared (grilled, fried, baked, boiled). Understanding these descriptions helps you order at restaurants, cook with others, and have casual conversations about meals.

Different cultures have different food traditions, and discussing these differences is a great way to learn about English-speaking countries. For example, breakfast in England often includes bacon and eggs, while in America, pancakes and cereal are common. By learning food vocabulary, you're also learning about culture and daily life. Don't be afraid to ask questions about unfamiliar foods or dishes—native speakers often enjoy explaining their food culture and traditions.

terms.definition: An edible plant or plant part like carrots, broccoli, or lettuce (野菜)
Sweet produce from plants, like apples, bananas, or oranges (果物)
Nutrient-rich food like chicken, fish, beef, or beans (タンパク質)
Products made from milk: cheese, yogurt, butter (乳製品)
Tasting very good; extremely pleasant to eat (おいしい)
Having a strong, hot flavor from spices like chili or pepper (辛い)
What you like or choose more than something else (好み)
To make something ready, especially food for eating (調理する)

sections.title: Food Talk Essentials
items: Food preferences: Use 'I like/love/enjoy,' 'I don't like,' 'I hate,' 'My favorite is...'
Taste descriptions: delicious, tasty, sweet, salty, bitter, sour, spicy, mild, bland.
Cooking methods: grilled, fried, baked, boiled, roasted, steamed (過去分詞形を使う).

questions: List your three favorite foods and three foods you dislike. For each, write a sentence explaining why using taste descriptors.
Imagine you're at a restaurant. Write a conversation where you ask about three dishes and tell the waiter what you'd like to order.

sections.content: Lesson 8: Colors & Descriptions - Painting a Picture with Words

Colors are fundamental to how we describe the world around us. The basic colors in English are red, blue, yellow, green, orange, purple, pink, brown, black, white, and gray. Beyond these primary and secondary colors, we can also describe colors with more specific terms: navy (dark blue), crimson (deep red), turquoise (blue-green), or coral (orange-pink). Colors aren't just nouns—they're often used as adjectives to describe objects: "a red apple," "blue eyes," "white clouds." Learning color vocabulary opens up new possibilities for describing people, places, things, and emotions.

Colors also carry cultural and emotional meanings in English. We say someone is "feeling blue" when they're sad, "seeing red" when they're angry, or "green with envy" when they're jealous. These color idioms (expressions that don't literally mean what they say) are common in English, and understanding them helps you sound more natural. You can describe not just the color itself, but also its intensity: a light blue, a dark red, a bright yellow, a pale pink. The more specific you can be with colors, the more vivid and interesting your descriptions become.

terms.definition: The visual property of objects resulting from light: red, blue, green, etc. (色)
Colors that cannot be made by mixing others: red, yellow, blue (基本色)
Colors made by mixing primary colors: orange, green, purple (二次色)
A phrase or expression whose meaning is not the literal meaning of the words (慣用句)
A word that describes a noun or person (形容詞)
The degree or strength of something, like brightness of a color (強度)
Bright, strong, and producing clear mental images (鮮やか・生き生きした)
Light in color; not bright or strong (淡い)

sections.title: Color & Description Power
items: Colors as adjectives come before nouns: 'a blue car,' 'red flowers,' 'white walls.'
Add intensity: light/dark/bright/pale + color = 'light blue,' 'dark green,' 'bright yellow.'
Color idioms are common: 'feeling blue' (sad), 'seeing red' (angry), 'green with envy' (jealous).

questions: Look around your room and describe five objects using color adjectives in complete sentences.
What color would you use to describe these emotions? Explain your choices: happiness, sadness, anger, calm, excitement.

sections.content: Lesson 9: Clothes & Fashion - Describing What You Wear

Clothing is a practical and interesting topic in English. Basic clothing items include shirts, T-shirts, pants/trousers, skirts, dresses, jackets, shoes, socks, and hats. You can describe clothes using colors, materials, and styles: "I'm wearing a blue cotton shirt," "She has a nice leather jacket," "He's in formal business attire." Different contexts require different types of clothing—formal clothes for job interviews or weddings, casual clothes for everyday activities, and athletic wear for sports. Understanding clothing vocabulary helps you shop, discuss fashion, and talk about what you wear in different situations.

Fashion is also a cultural topic. What's considered formal, casual, or trendy varies greatly between countries and even within different regions. In some English-speaking countries, people dress very casually for work, while in others, business formal dress is expected. Learning about these differences helps you understand cultural norms better. You can also express your personal style: "I prefer comfortable clothes," "I like minimalist fashion," "I love bold colors." Whether you're fashion-conscious or simply practical, this vocabulary is useful for everyday communication.

terms.definition: Things you wear to cover your body (衣服)
What something is made of: cotton, silk, wool, leather, etc. (素材)
A distinctive way something looks or is done (スタイル)
Proper and serious in dress or manner (正装の)
Relaxed and informal in dress or manner (カジュアルな)
Clothing designed for sports and physical activity (スポーツウェア)
Following the latest fashions or trends (流行の)
What you choose or like more than something else (好み)

sections.title: Clothing Descriptions
items: Basic sentence: 'I'm wearing [color] [material] [clothing item].' Example: 'I'm wearing blue cotton pants.'
Mix and match descriptions: color + material + style. 'A black leather jacket.' 'Pink silk dress.'
Use 'formal,' 'casual,' 'business,' 'athletic' to describe clothing contexts and appropriateness.

questions: Describe what you're wearing today using at least color, material, and style for each item.
What clothes would you wear to these events? Explain your choices: (a) job interview, (b) beach day, (c) formal dinner, (d) gym session.

sections.content: Lesson 10: Verbs of Daily Life - Talking About What You Do

Verbs are the action words that make communication come alive. In this lesson, we focus on common verbs that describe your daily activities: wake up, get up, eat, drink, shower, dress, work, study, play, sleep, watch, read, and relax. These verbs follow patterns that are essential for telling others about your day. In the present simple tense, we use the base form for most subjects: "I wake up at 7 AM," "You work hard," "They play football." However, with third person singular (he, she, it), we add an 's': "She wakes up early," "He plays guitar." Understanding these patterns helps you describe your routine confidently.

Beyond just knowing the verbs, understanding their usage in context matters. Some verbs are followed by other verbs in the gerund form (verb+ing): "I enjoy reading," "She likes playing tennis." Others are followed by the infinitive (to+verb): "I want to study English," "He needs to sleep." Mastering these verb patterns opens up more complex conversations about your life, goals, and daily habits. Regular practice will help these patterns become automatic, so you can focus on meaningful communication rather than grammar rules.

terms.definition: A word that shows action or state of being (動詞)
Tense used for habits, routines, and general facts (現在形)
Grammar form used with 'he,' 'she,' 'it' (requiring -s on the verb) (三人称単数)
A verb form ending in -ing used as a noun (動名詞)
The base form of a verb, often with 'to': to go, to eat, to sleep (不定詞)
A regular or usual way of doing things; a habit (日常・習慣)
To rest and become calm and less worried (リラックスする)
A repeated or regular way something is done (パターン)

sections.title: Daily Life Verb Patterns
items: Simple present: 'I/you/we/they work' BUT 'he/she/it works' (add -s for 3rd person singular).
Verbs + gerund: 'I enjoy reading,' 'She likes playing,' 'They hate waiting.'
Verbs + infinitive: 'I want to sleep,' 'He needs to study,' 'They plan to travel.'

questions: Write your typical daily schedule using at least 8 different verbs from this lesson. Use present simple tense.
Complete these sentences with either the gerund (-ing) or infinitive (to+verb) form: (a) I enjoy ___. (b) He wants ___. (c) They like ___. (d) She needs ___.

sections.content: Lesson 11: Hobbies & Interests - Sharing What You Love

Hobbies and interests are wonderful conversation starters. They tell us what people enjoy and what makes them happy outside of work or school. Common hobbies include reading, drawing, cooking, gardening, collecting things, playing sports, gaming, watching movies, listening to music, and traveling. In English, we often use "I'm interested in..." or "I'm passionate about..." to express strong feelings toward a hobby. We also use structures like "I enjoy..." (followed by gerund) or "I like to..." (followed by infinitive). Understanding this vocabulary helps you bond with others over shared interests and express your personality.

Talking about hobbies also allows you to discuss why something interests you. Maybe you enjoy reading because it helps you relax, or you love cooking because you can share food with family. These reasons make conversation more interesting and personal. In professional settings, hobbies can also be relevant—they might show creativity, discipline, leadership, or teamwork skills. By building a rich vocabulary around your interests, you create opportunities for meaningful conversations and connections with others learning English.

terms.definition: An activity or interest that you do for pleasure in your free time (趣味・ホビー)
A strong curiosity or concern about something (興味)
Having or showing strong feelings and enthusiasm (情熱的な)
To take pleasure in something; to like something (楽しむ)
To gather items together as a hobby; stamps, coins, etc. (集める)
The hobby of growing flowers, vegetables, or other plants (園芸)
To rest and become calm and free of stress (くつろぐ)
To form a close relationship with someone through shared interests (絆を深める)

sections.title: Hobby Talk Structures
items: Expression options: 'I love...,' 'I'm interested in...,' 'I'm passionate about...,' 'I enjoy + gerund.'
Add reasons: 'I enjoy reading because it helps me relax.' 'I love cooking because I can share food.'
In professional contexts, hobbies can show positive qualities like creativity, discipline, or teamwork.

questions: List three hobbies you have or would like to have. For each, write one sentence using a different expression structure.
Interview a partner about their hobbies: Ask what they enjoy, why they enjoy it, and how often they do it. Report back in 3-4 sentences.

sections.content: Lesson 12: Places Around Us - Describing Locations

Every day, we move between different places: homes, schools, offices, stores, parks, restaurants, and hospitals. Knowing how to name and describe these places in English is essential for giving directions, making plans, and discussing where you've been. We can describe places using adjectives: a beautiful park, a busy street, a quiet library, a crowded shopping mall. We can also say what we do in each place: "We eat at a restaurant," "We borrow books at a library," "We buy groceries at a supermarket." Understanding the relationship between places and their functions helps you make logical connections and speak naturally.

Beyond just naming places, you can describe their characteristics: size (large, small, spacious, cramped), atmosphere (quiet, noisy, peaceful, lively), and condition (clean, dirty, well-maintained, run-down). You can also describe your feelings about places: "I love this park because it's peaceful," "The restaurant is too noisy," "The library is my favorite place to study." Describing places well helps listeners visualize what you're talking about and creates more engaging conversations. This skill becomes even more important when giving directions or recommending places to others.

terms.definition: A physical location or area (場所)
Large and having plenty of space (広々とした)
Small and without much space; uncomfortably crowded (狭い)
The feeling or mood of a place (雰囲気)
Calm, quiet, and free from disturbance (平和な・穏やかな)
Full of energy, excitement, and movement (活気のある)
Kept clean and in good working condition (よく管理されている)
To form a picture or image in your mind (想像する)

sections.title: Describing Places Effectively
items: Basic formula: 'The [place] is [adjective]. We [action] there.' Example: 'The park is beautiful. We play there.'
Atmosphere adjectives: peaceful, noisy, lively, quiet, crowded, calm, busy, relaxing.
Use 'because' to explain why you like or dislike a place: 'I love this cafe because it's quiet and cozy.'

questions: Think of three places you visit regularly. Write 2-3 sentences about each describing what it's like and what you do there.
Describe your favorite place using at least five adjectives. Then explain why you like it so much.

sections.content: Lesson 13: Weather & Seasons - Talking About the Environment

Weather is one of the most universal conversation topics across languages. In English, we have specific vocabulary for different weather conditions: sunny, rainy, cloudy, snowy, windy, hot, cold, warm, cool, humid, and dry. We often start conversations with "How's the weather?" or "It's a beautiful day today!" The verb "to be" is commonly used with weather descriptions: "It's sunny," "It's cold," "It's raining." Note that in English, unlike some other languages, we use "it" for weather rather than personalizing the weather. You can also use the present continuous tense (is/are + -ing) for weather happening now: "It's raining," "It's getting colder."

Each season brings different weather patterns and activities. Spring brings rain and flowers, summer brings hot weather and outdoor activities, fall/autumn brings cooler temperatures and changing leaves, and winter brings cold and snow. Understanding how seasons and weather affect daily life helps you discuss plans and adjust your language based on context. For example, you might dress differently, plan different activities, or have different emotional responses to weather. Building a strong weather vocabulary helps you make small talk naturally and describe conditions you're experiencing in real time.

terms.definition: The condition of the atmosphere at a specific time (天気)
Bright and clear with no clouds; shining with sunlight (晴れた)
Characterized by rain; wet (雨の)
Having a lot of moisture in the air; damp (湿度の高い)
Characterized by strong air movement; breezy (風の強い)
One of the four periods of the year: spring, summer, fall, winter (季節)
A prediction of what the weather will be like (天気予報)
How hot or cold something is, measured in degrees (気温)

sections.title: Weather Talk Essentials
items: Basic structure: 'It's [weather adjective].' Example: 'It's sunny.' 'It's cold.' 'It's raining.'
Present continuous for ongoing weather: 'It's raining,' 'It's getting warmer,' 'It's becoming cloudier.'
Small talk opener: 'How's the weather?' or 'Nice day, isn't it?' to start conversations naturally.

questions: Describe the weather today and predict tomorrow's weather using at least three weather adjectives for each.
What activities do you like to do in each season? Write 2-3 sentences explaining your seasonal preferences and how weather affects your plans.

sections.content: Lesson 14: Simple Present Tense - Describing Habits & Routines

The simple present tense is one of the most fundamental tenses in English. It's used for habits, routines, facts, and general truths that don't change. The structure is straightforward: subject + verb (base form). However, there's an important rule: when the subject is third person singular (he, she, it), we add an 's' or 'es' to the verb. For example: "I eat breakfast every morning" (no change), but "She eats breakfast every morning" (add 's'). Common adverbs of frequency (always, usually, often, sometimes, seldom, never) are placed before the main verb or after the verb 'to be' and help show how regularly something happens.

Understanding when to use the simple present is crucial. Use it to describe your daily routine: "I wake up at 7 AM, shower, get dressed, and eat breakfast." Use it for facts and general statements: "The Earth revolves around the Sun." Use it for instructions: "You mix the flour and eggs together." And use it for scheduled future events: "The train leaves at 3 PM tomorrow." By mastering this tense, you can talk about most of your everyday life and communicate in a clear, understandable way. Regular practice will make the third-person 's' automatic, so you can focus on what you're saying rather than how you're saying it.

terms.definition: Tense for habits, routines, facts, and permanent situations (現在形)
Grammatical form requiring -s or -es on the verb when subject is 'he,' 'she,' or 'it' (三人称単数)
Words showing how often something happens: always, usually, often, sometimes, never (頻度の副詞)
Something you do regularly and repeatedly (習慣)
A regular sequence of actions you do every day (日常・日課)
Something known to be true; reality (事実)
Information telling how to do something (指示・説明)
Arranged or set for a particular time (予定された)

sections.title: Simple Present Rules & Uses
items: Base rule: I/you/we/they + verb. He/she/it + verb + s/es. ('She likes coffee.' 'He watches movies.')
Frequency words go before the main verb: 'I always eat breakfast.' 'She sometimes arrives late.'
Uses: habits ('I exercise daily'), facts ('Water boils at 100°C'), routines, scheduled events.

questions: Write 5 sentences about what you do every day using the simple present tense. Include at least one sentence with a frequency adverb.
Write sentences with these subjects in simple present: (a) I (b) She (c) They (d) It (e) He. Notice how the verbs change.

sections.content: Lesson 15: Present Continuous - Describing What's Happening Now

While the simple present describes habits and routines, the present continuous tense describes actions happening right now, at this very moment. The structure is: subject + to be (am, is, are) + verb + -ing. For example: "I am reading," "She is studying," "They are playing football." This tense is called "continuous" because it emphasizes that the action is ongoing and not yet finished. It's different from the simple present in an important way: "I read books" (habit, I read often) versus "I am reading a book" (right now, this specific moment, the action is in progress).

The present continuous is also used for temporary situations (short-term actions or changes): "She is living in Tokyo for six months" (temporary stay), or planned near-future events: "We are going to the movies tonight." One important rule: some verbs are rarely used in continuous form because they describe states rather than actions. These are called stative verbs, like 'be,' 'have,' 'like,' 'want,' 'know,' 'understand,' and 'believe.' You'd say "I like pizza" not "I am liking pizza." Understanding when to use the present continuous versus simple present helps you describe situations more accurately and naturally.

terms.definition: Tense for actions happening right now; structure is am/is/are + verb-ing (現在進行形)
Continuing to happen; not finished (継続中の)
Lasting for a limited time; not permanent (一時的な)
A verb describing a state or condition rather than an action (状態動詞)
The -ing form of a verb used as a noun or with continuous tenses (動名詞)
A verb that describes a physical or mental action (動作動詞)
At the present moment; immediately (今このとき)
Currently happening; not yet completed (進行中)

sections.title: Present Continuous Guidelines
items: Structure: am/is/are + verb-ing. 'I am studying.' 'She is writing.' 'They are playing.'
Use for right-now actions, temporary situations, and near-future plans.
Remember: Stative verbs (be, have, like, want, know) are NOT used in continuous form.

questions: Look around you right now. Write 5 sentences describing what people or things are doing using present continuous. What is happening at this moment?
Rewrite these simple present sentences as present continuous and explain the difference: (a) I eat lunch. (b) She writes. (c) They watch TV.

sections.content: Lesson 16: Have & Has - Talking About Possession & Experience

The verbs "have" and "has" are essential for talking about possession (what you own) and experience (what you've done). For possession, we use "have" with I, you, we, and they, and "has" with he, she, and it: "I have a cat," "She has a brother," "They have a house." Beyond just ownership, "have" describes family members, physical characteristics, and possessions: "He has brown eyes," "She has two children," "I have a bicycle." This basic usage is straightforward and essential for daily communication.

Another important use of "have/has" is talking about experience when combined with a past participle: "I have visited Paris," "She has eaten sushi," "They have lived here for five years." This form, called the present perfect, shows that something happened in the past but is relevant to the present. While we'll study the present perfect more formally in higher levels, understanding "have" and "has" now gives you the foundation. Additionally, "have" is used for activities or possessions necessary for survival: "I have to study," "He has to work," "She has to eat." Mastering these uses of "have/has" opens up many possibilities for expressing yourself in English.

terms.definition: Verb showing possession or ownership (持っている)
Something you own; what belongs to you (所有・所持)
Things that have happened to you; your background (経験)
Features of your body like height, hair color, eye color (身体的特徴)
Tense connecting past and present; what you've done (現在完了形)
Form of verb used with 'have': gone, eaten, lived (過去分詞)
Connected to the present; important to the current situation (関連する)
Needed; essential; must have (必要な)

sections.title: Have/Has Usage Patterns
items: Possession: 'I have...' (with I, you, we, they) vs. 'She has...' (with he, she, it).
Physical traits: 'I have black hair.' 'He has blue eyes.' 'They have freckles.'
Obligation (have to/has to): 'I have to study.' 'She has to work.' (means it's necessary/required)

questions: Write sentences describing what you and three other people have (possessions, family members, physical traits). Use both 'have' and 'has.'
Complete these sentences: (a) I have ___. (b) My friend has ___. (c) My teacher has ___. (d) They have ___.

sections.content: Lesson 17: Negations & Questions - Saying "No" and Asking Effectively

Being able to say "no" and ask questions is just as important as making positive statements. To make negative statements in simple present, we use "do not" (don't) or "does not" (doesn't) before the main verb: "I don't like coffee," "She doesn't eat meat," "They don't play tennis." Notice that when we use "don't" or "doesn't," the main verb returns to its base form—we don't add an 's' to the verb when using "doesn't." For yes/no questions, we put "do" or "does" at the beginning: "Do you like pizza?" "Does he speak French?" This inverted word order signals that we're asking a question.

With the verb "to be," negations and questions work differently. We don't use "do/does." Instead, we place "not" directly after the verb: "I'm not tired," "She is not happy," "They aren't here." For questions with "to be," we invert: "Are you ready?" "Is she coming?" "Is it raining?" With "have/has," the process is similar to "to be" in modern English: "I haven't seen that," "Has she arrived?" Understanding these patterns for negation and questions is crucial because you'll use them constantly in real conversations. Native speakers ask questions far more than they make statements, so mastering question formation is essential.

terms.definition: Expressing that something is not true or doesn't happen (否定)
Words used to make negative sentences in simple present (...ない)
Negative form for third person singular subjects (...ない)
A question that can be answered with 'yes' or 'no' (はい/いいえの質問)
To switch the usual word order; put the verb or helper word first (逆にする)
A shortened form of two words: don't, doesn't, isn't, aren't (短縮形)
The simplest form of a verb: go, eat, run, sleep (基本形)
A verb that helps form tenses: do, does, have, has, will (助動詞)

sections.title: Negation & Question Patterns
items: Simple present negation: 'I don't like...' and 'She doesn't want...' (use base form verb after don't/doesn't).
Simple present questions: 'Do you have...?' and 'Does he know...?' (do/does at the beginning).
To be negation & questions don't use do/does: 'I'm not happy.' 'Are you tired?' 'Is she coming?'

questions: Turn these statements into negations and questions: (a) You like chocolate. (b) She studies Spanish. (c) They live in Tokyo.
Write 3 yes/no questions you would ask a new classmate to get to know them better. Use different question structures.

sections.content: Lesson 18: Adjectives & Adverbs - Adding Descriptive Power

Adjectives and adverbs are the words that add color and detail to your descriptions. Adjectives describe nouns (people, places, things): "a beautiful day," "an intelligent student," "delicious food." In English, adjectives typically come before the noun they describe: "I have a red car" not "I have a car red." After linking verbs like "to be," adjectives can come after the noun: "The day is beautiful," "She is intelligent." Adverbs, on the other hand, describe verbs, adjectives, or other adverbs: "She runs quickly," "That movie is very interesting," "He works extremely hard." Many adverbs are formed by adding '-ly' to an adjective: quick → quickly, slow → slowly, beautiful → beautifully.

One common mistake beginners make is using adjectives where adverbs are needed, or vice versa. Remember: "She speaks good English" is incorrect (good is an adjective); "She speaks English well" is correct (well is an adverb describing how she speaks). Another mistake is placing adverbs incorrectly. Most adverbs go after the verb or before the adjective they modify: "She always arrives early" (not "She arrives always early"). Learning the difference between adjectives and adverbs, and using them correctly, makes your English sound more natural and precise. It also helps you express ideas more effectively and convey exactly what you mean.

terms.definition: A word that describes a noun (person, place, thing) (形容詞)
A word that describes a verb, adjective, or other adverb; often ends in -ly (副詞)
A word representing a person, place, thing, or idea (名詞)
A verb that connects a subject with a description: is, seems, appears, feels, tastes (連結動詞)
To describe or change the meaning of another word (修飾する)
Two adjectives joined together: dark-blue, well-known (複合形容詞)
Where a word goes in a sentence (置き場・位置)
Exact and accurate; clear and specific (正確な)

sections.title: Adjective & Adverb Rules
items: Adjectives describe nouns and usually come BEFORE the noun: 'a beautiful house,' 'smart students.'
After 'to be': adjectives come AFTER: 'The house is beautiful.' 'I am happy.'
Form many adverbs by adding -ly to adjectives: quick→quickly, slow→slowly, careful→carefully.

questions: Write 5 sentences using adjectives to describe people, places, or things. Pay attention to placement before or after the noun.
Convert these adjectives to adverbs and write a sentence with each: (a) quick (b) slow (c) beautiful (d) careful (e) happy

sections.content: Lesson 19: Prepositions - Understanding Location & Time

Prepositions are small words that show relationships between other words, typically showing location, direction, or time. Common prepositions of location include: in, on, at, under, over, between, beside, behind, in front of. For example: "The book is on the table," "She's sitting in a chair," "The picture is on the wall." The challenge with prepositions is that they don't always translate directly from one language to another. In English, we say "on time" (not "in time" when meaning punctual), "in the morning" (not "on the morning"), and "at school" (not "in school" for current attendance). These are often learned through exposure and practice rather than rules.

Prepositions of time are equally important. We use "in" for months and years ("in January"), "on" for specific days ("on Monday"), and "at" for specific times ("at 3 PM"). The phrase "for" shows duration: "I've lived here for five years." Prepositions can be tricky because there are many exceptions, and native speakers don't always follow consistent logic. The best approach is to learn prepositions in common phrases and chunks: "at work," "in the morning," "at night," "on the bus," "in bed." By memorizing these useful phrases, you can communicate effectively without needing to understand every rule.

terms.definition: A word showing the relationship between a noun and other words (前置詞)
Where something is; its physical position (場所・位置)
The way something is moving or facing (方向)
How long something lasts or continues (期間)
To convert words from one language to another (翻訳する)
A case that doesn't follow the regular rule (例外)
A group of words that often go together (チャンク・まとまり)
Following the natural usage patterns of native speakers (慣用的な)

sections.title: Common Preposition Patterns
items: Location: in (enclosed space), on (surface), at (specific place). 'In a room,' 'on a table,' 'at school.'
Time: in (months/years/seasons), on (days/dates), at (specific times). 'In January,' 'on Monday,' 'at 3 PM.'
Useful chunks to memorize: 'at work,' 'in the morning,' 'on the weekend,' 'at night,' 'in front of,' 'behind.'

questions: Write 5 sentences describing where objects or people are using different prepositions of location.
Write sentences about your daily schedule using prepositions of time: when you wake up, eat, study, sleep, etc.

sections.content: Lesson 20: Combining Skills - Having a Simple Conversation

Throughout these first 20 lessons, you've built a foundation in English fundamentals. Now it's time to bring it all together and have a simple conversation. A typical conversation between two people who just met might start with greetings and introductions: "Hello, my name is..." or "Hi, I'm..." Then you might ask questions about the other person: "Where are you from?" "What do you do?" "What are your hobbies?" The person answers using present simple tense, and then you ask follow-up questions. For example: "I enjoy reading." → "What kind of books do you like?" → "I like mystery novels." This simple exchange-question-answer pattern is the foundation of all conversation.

When having conversations, remember these key points: Listen actively to what the other person says, ask follow-up questions to show interest, use the vocabulary you've learned from previous lessons, and don't worry about perfection. Native speakers are much more interested in communication and understanding than in perfect grammar. If you make a mistake, don't stop the conversation to correct yourself—just keep talking. You can also ask for help if you need it: "How do you say this in English?" or "Can you repeat that, please?" By combining the skills from all 20 lessons—introductions, questions, description, and past/present tenses—you can engage in meaningful conversations and continue learning from real interactions.

terms.definition: An exchange of words between two or more people (会話)
A polite word or phrase used when meeting someone (あいさつ)
Another question based on what someone just said (フォローアップ質問)
To listen carefully and show interest in what someone is saying (積極的に聞く)
Back-and-forth communication; sharing of information (交換・やり取り)
Having real importance or relevance (有意義な)
To speak and listen with others; to communicate (交流する)
Being completely correct or without flaws (完璧)

sections.title: Conversation Strategy & Tips
items: Pattern: Greeting → Introduction → Questions → Listening → Follow-up Questions → Topic Shifts.
Keep it simple: Use present simple, short questions, and familiar vocabulary from these 20 lessons.
Don't aim for perfection—focus on communication. Native speakers value effort and understanding over grammar accuracy.

questions: Role-play: Write a conversation between two people meeting for the first time. Include greetings, introductions, questions, and at least one topic shift (e.g., from hobbies to family).
With a study partner, practice having a 5-minute conversation covering: where you're from, what you do, hobbies, family, and weather. Record yourself and review for pronunciation and flow.
